Regions¶

class bosl2.regions.Path(points=(), closed=True)[source]¶

Bases: Distributable, Extrudable, Roundable, list

A 2-D path: a list of [x, y] points, with every path operation as a method.

Subclasses list deliberately – the same trick as base_bgtk.Vec3. Every place that already treats a path as a plain point list (indexing, iteration, len(), equality with a plain list, and crossing the native polygon()/FFI boundary) keeps working, so this is a drop-in for the raw lists the toolkit passes around, while giving the chained object form for new code:

Path([[0, 0], [80, 0], [80, 60], [0, 60]]).offset(r=-2).round_corners(radius=1).polygon()

Because it IS a list, isinstance(x, Path) is the type check that replaced the old is_path()/is_region() guards. Every method returns a NEW Path (or list/array) – nothing mutates in place, so a path can be reused as the base for several derived outlines.

All of BOSL2’s paths.scad operations are methods here; there are no module-level path functions. The public ergonomic operations are instance methods/properties. The numeric kernels and graph algorithms are private @staticmethod``s (``_path_length, _path_cut_points, …) so they can also be applied to raw point arrays that are not Path instances – notably the 3-D paths that only bosl2/shapes3d.py’s path_text() handles, which this 2-D class cannot wrap.

Parameters:

  • points (Sequence) – the [x, y] points (anything array-like; numpy scalars are converted to float)

  • closed (bool) – whether the path is a closed polygon (default True)

Examples

A box outline inset by the wall thickness and with rounded corners, extruded into a plate:

outline = Path([[0, 0], [80, 0], [80, 60], [0, 60]])
plate = outline.offset(r=-3).round_corners(radius=5).polygon().linear_extrude(height=4)
plate.show()

⬇ Download STL mesh

property array: ndarray¶

The points as an (N, 2) numpy array, for doing your own vectorised maths.

bounds()[source]¶

[[min_x, min_y], [max_x, max_y]].

Return type:

ndarray

property width: float¶
property length_y: float¶
area(signed=False)[source]¶

Enclosed area; signed keeps the sign (negative == clockwise).

Parameters:

signed (bool)

Return type:

float

is_clockwise()[source]¶
Return type:

bool

perimeter()[source]¶

Total length around the path.

Return type:

float

length()¶

Total length around the path.

Return type:

float

segment_lengths()[source]¶

Length of each segment, as an ndarray.

Return type:

ndarray

length_fractions()[source]¶

Cumulative length fraction at each point, as an ndarray.

Return type:

ndarray

contains(point)[source]¶

True if point is inside the closed polygon (on the boundary counts as inside).

Containment is only meaningful for a closed polygon, so an open path (closed=False) always returns False rather than testing.

Parameters:

point (Sequence[float])

Return type:

bool

property is_closed: bool¶

True if the first and last points of the path coincide.

is_simple()[source]¶

True if the path does not self-intersect.

Return type:

bool

closest_point(pt)[source]¶

[SEGNUM, POINT]: the closest path segment to pt, and the closest point on it.

Parameters:

pt (Sequence[float])

Return type:

list

tangents(uniform=True)[source]¶

Unit tangent at each point, as an ndarray.

Parameters:

uniform (bool)

Return type:

ndarray

normals(tangents=None)[source]¶

Unit normal at each point, as an ndarray.

Return type:

ndarray

curvature()[source]¶

Curvature at each point, as an ndarray.

Return type:

ndarray

torsion()[source]¶

Numeric torsion estimate of a 3-D path at each point, as an ndarray.

Return type:

ndarray

cut_points(cutdist, direction=False)[source]¶

Point(s) at the given distance(s) along the path.

Parameters:

direction (bool)

offset(r=None, delta=None, chamfer=False, _fn=None, _fa=None, _fs=None)[source]¶

Offset by r (rounded joins) or delta (sharp/chamfered). See _offset().

Prefer .polygon().offset(...) (native, Manifold-side) when you only need geometry; this is for when the result is needed as points.

Parameters:

  • r (float | None)

  • delta (float | None)

  • chamfer (bool)

  • _fn (float | None)

  • _fa (float | None)

  • _fs (float | None)

Return type:

Path

merge_collinear()[source]¶

Drop points that lie on a straight run.

Return type:

Path

close()[source]¶

Append the start point if the path isn’t already closed.

Return type:

Path

cleanup()[source]¶

Drop a duplicate closing point if present.

Return type:

Path

reversed_path()[source]¶

The same outline wound the other way.

Return type:

Path

deduplicated()[source]¶

Drop consecutive repeated points (_deduplicate()).

Return type:

Path

subdivide(**kwargs)[source]¶

Insert points along the path.

Parameters:

kwargs (Any)

Return type:

Path

resample(**kwargs)[source]¶

Resample to evenly spaced points.

Parameters:

kwargs (Any)

Return type:

Path

cut(cutdist)[source]¶

Split the path at the given distance(s), returning the sub-paths.

Return type:

list[Path]

split_at_self_crossings(eps=1e-09)[source]¶

Split this 2-D path into subpaths wherever it crosses itself.

Parameters:

eps (float)

Return type:

list[Path]

polygon_parts(nonzero=False, eps=1e-09)[source]¶

Split a possibly self-intersecting polygon into non-intersecting simple polygons.

Parameters:

  • nonzero (bool)

  • eps (float)

Return type:

list[Path]

translate(v)[source]¶

Translate every point by v (2-D; a 1-vector shifts X only).

Parameters:

v (Sequence[float])

Return type:

Path

move(v)¶

Translate every point by v (2-D; a 1-vector shifts X only).

Parameters:

v (Sequence[float])

Return type:

Path

rot(a)[source]¶

Rotate every point by a degrees about the origin (Z axis).

Parameters:

a (float)

Return type:

Path

rotate(a)¶

Rotate every point by a degrees about the origin (Z axis).

Parameters:

a (float)

Return type:

Path

mirror(v)[source]¶

Reflect every point across the line through the origin with normal v.

Parameters:

v (Sequence[float])

Return type:

Path

yflip(y=0.0)[source]¶

Reflect every point across the horizontal line Y=*y* (default: the X axis).

Parameters:

y (float)

Return type:

Path

right(x)[source]¶

Translate by x along +X.

Parameters:

x (float)

Return type:

Path

left(x)[source]¶

Translate by x along -X.

Parameters:

x (float)

Return type:

Path

back(y)[source]¶

Translate by y along +Y.

Parameters:

y (float)

Return type:

Path

forward(y)[source]¶

Translate by y along -Y (BOSL2 fwd()).

Parameters:

y (float)

Return type:

Path

fwd(y)¶

Translate by y along -Y (BOSL2 fwd()).

Parameters:

y (float)

Return type:

Path

to_region()[source]¶

This path as a single-outline Region.

polygon()[source]¶

Native 2-D geometry for this path (crosses the FFI as plain floats).

geometry()[source]¶

Native 2-D geometry – the name Region also exposes, so a caller that may hold either a Path or a Region can ask for geometry without checking which it got.

stroke(width=1, closed=None, **kwargs)[source]¶

Draw this path as a solid line of the given width (see bosl2.drawing.stroke()).

Parameters:

  • width (float)

  • closed (bool | None)

  • kwargs (Any)

dashed_stroke(dashpat=(3, 3), closed=None, **kwargs)[source]¶

Break this path into dash sub-paths (see bosl2.drawing.dashed_stroke()).

Parameters:

  • dashpat (Sequence[float])

  • closed (bool | None)

  • kwargs (Any)

Return type:

list[Path]

class bosl2.regions.Path3D(points=(), closed=True)[source]¶

Bases: Distributable, Extrudable, Roundable, list

A 3-D path: a list of [x, y, z] points, with the path operations that make sense in 3-D.

The 3-D counterpart of Path. Like Path it subclasses list (so it stays a drop-in for the raw 3-D point lists the sweep/loft functions consume), and every method returns a NEW object. It carries the dimension-independent measurements (length, segment lengths, tangents, normals(), curvature, torsion()), resampling/subdividing/cutting, and the 3-D transforms (translate/move, right/left/back/forward/up/down, scale, mirror, rotate). The inherently-2-D operations of Path (polygon, area, offset, round_corners, point-in-polygon) are intentionally absent; use path2d() to drop to the XY plane when you want them.

Parameters:

  • points (Sequence) – the [x, y, z] points (anything array-like; numpy scalars are converted to float)

  • closed (bool) – whether the path is a closed loop (default True)

Examples

A helix resampled to fewer points and swept into a coil:

coil = helix(turns=3, h=60, r=20).resample(n=120)
coil.stroke(width=4).show()

⬇ Download STL mesh

property array: ndarray¶

The points as an (N, 3) numpy array, for doing your own vectorised maths.

bounds()[source]¶

[[min_x, min_y, min_z], [max_x, max_y, max_z]].

Return type:

ndarray

perimeter()[source]¶

Total length along the path.

Return type:

float

Total length along the path.

Return type:

float

Length of each segment, as an ndarray.

Return type:

ndarray

Cumulative length fraction at each point, as an ndarray.

Return type:

ndarray

list

Return type:

ndarray

Unit normal at each point (in the local plane of the curve), as an ndarray.

ndarray

Curvature at each point, as an ndarray.

ndarray

ndarray

translate(v)[source]¶

Translate every point by v (a shorter vector pads with zeros).

Parameters:

v (Sequence[float])

Return type:

Path3D

move(v)¶

Translate every point by v (a shorter vector pads with zeros).

Parameters:

v (Sequence[float])

Return type:

Path3D

scale(v)[source]¶

Scale every point by a scalar or a per-axis [sx, sy, sz] factor.

Parameters:

v (float | Sequence[float])

Return type:

Path3D

rotate(a, v=None)[source]¶

Rotate the points. rotate(ang, axis) spins about axis; rotate(ang) about +Z; rotate([rx, ry, rz]) applies the OpenSCAD X-then-Y-then-Z Euler rotation.

Parameters:

  • a (float | Sequence[float])

  • v (Sequence[float] | None)

Return type:

Path3D

rot(a, v=None)¶

Rotate the points. rotate(ang, axis) spins about axis; rotate(ang) about +Z; rotate([rx, ry, rz]) applies the OpenSCAD X-then-Y-then-Z Euler rotation.

Parameters:

  • a (float | Sequence[float])

  • v (Sequence[float] | None)

Return type:

Path3D

mirror(v)[source]¶

Reflect every point across the plane through the origin with normal v.

Parameters:

v (Sequence[float])

Return type:

Path3D

x (float)

Path3D

Parameters:

x (float)

Path3D

y (float)

Path3D

y (float)

Path3D

fwd(y)¶

Translate by y along -Y (BOSL2 fwd()).

Parameters:

y (float)

Return type:

Path3D

up(z)[source]¶

Translate by z along +Z.

Parameters:

z (float)

Return type:

Path3D

down(z)[source]¶

Translate by z along -Z.

Parameters:

z (float)

Return type:

Path3D

path2d()[source]¶

Drop the Z coordinate, giving a 2-D Path (the XY projection).

Return type:

Path

stroke(width=1, closed=None, **kwargs)[source]¶

Draw this 3-D path as a solid tube of the given width (see bosl2.drawing.stroke()).

Parameters:

  • width (float)

  • closed (bool | None)

  • kwargs (Any)

dashed_stroke(dashpat=(3, 3), closed=None, **kwargs)[source]¶

Break this 3-D path into dash sub-paths (see bosl2.drawing.dashed_stroke()).

Parameters:

  • dashpat (Sequence[float])

  • closed (bool | None)

  • kwargs (Any)

Return type:

list[Path3D]

class bosl2.regions.Region(paths=())[source]¶

Bases: list

A 2-D region: a list of Path outlines, holes included.

BOSL2 represents a shape-with-holes as a list of paths (outline first, then the holes), and that is what this is – so, like Path, it subclasses list and stays a drop-in for the raw region data the toolkit already passes to region()/union().

Parameters:

paths (Sequence) – the outlines; each is coerced to a Path. A single flat point list is accepted and treated as one outline.

Examples

A rectangular plate with a rectangular hole (outline + one hole), extruded into a solid:

region = Region.with_holes(
    [[0, 0], [80, 0], [80, 60], [0, 60]],
    [[20, 20], [60, 20], [60, 40], [20, 40]],
)
region.geometry().linear_extrude(height=5).show()

⬇ Download STL mesh

classmethod with_holes(outline, *holes)[source]¶

A region from an outline plus hole outlines.

This is what a concentric DifferenceWithOffset produces: outline + inner hole, no clipping involved.

Parameters:

  • outline (Sequence)

  • holes (Sequence)

Return type:

Region

property outline: Path¶

The outer path.

property holes: list[Path]¶
offset(r=None, delta=None, chamfer=False)[source]¶

Offset every path in the region.

Parameters:

  • r (float | None)

  • delta (float | None)

  • chamfer (bool)

Return type:

Region

round_corners(radius=None, **kwargs)[source]¶
Parameters:

  • radius (float | list[float] | None)

  • kwargs (Any)

Return type:

Region

translate(v)[source]¶
Parameters:

v (Sequence[float])

Return type:

Region

bounds()[source]¶

[[min_x, min_y], [max_x, max_y]] over every path.

Return type:

ndarray

geometry()[source]¶

Native 2-D geometry: the outline with the holes subtracted.

stroke(width=1, **kwargs)[source]¶

Draw every path in this region as a closed solid line (see bosl2.drawing.stroke()).

Parameters:

  • width (float)

  • kwargs (Any)

dashed_stroke(dashpat=(3, 3), **kwargs)[source]¶

Break every path in this region into dash sub-paths (see bosl2.drawing.dashed_stroke()).

Parameters:

  • dashpat (Sequence[float])

  • kwargs (Any)

Return type:

list[Path]
